coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot/carbon nitride composites fluorescent probe for the highly selective detection of Cr(VI) ions
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Zheng, Xiaochun
Ren, Shoutian
Gai, Qixiao
Liu, Wenjun
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescence quenching of carbon dots (CDs) has been widely used for ions detection, but it usually exhibits poor selectivity for specific ions, so how to enhance their response selectivity is still an urgent task. In this paper, CDs and carbon nitride (C3N4) nanosheets are prepared by pyrolysis and thermal oxidation method, respectively, followed by their ultrasonic mixing to synthesize CDs/C3N4 composites. They are characterized by transmission electron microscope, X-ray diffractometer, X-ray photoelectron spectrometer, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, UV–vis and fluorescence spectrophotometer. The synthesized CDs and C3N4 nanosheets both exhibit strong fluorescence response to multiple ions. As a comparison, CDs/C3N4 composites exhibit highly enhanced selectivity to Cr6+ as a result of partial consumption of -NH2 groups and CN bonds or their reduced binding ability. Meanwhile, our CDs/C3N4 probe exhibits high sensitivity of 54 %, short response time of less than 1 min, low detection limit of 0.39 μM, wide linear response interval of 2.0−80.0 μM and high stability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Carbon nitride
Fluorescence quenching
Functional groups
Chromium ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of CDs/C3N4 probe for Cr6+
Cr6+ detection of CDs/C3N4 in real samples
Cr6+ detection of CDs/C3N4-15 in real samples
Response mechanism of CDs/C3N4 to Cr6+

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Weihai Science and Technology Development Program

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology characterization of C3N4, CDs and CDs/C3N4-15. (a), (b) SEM and TEM images of C3N4, respectively, (c), (d) TEM images of CDs and CDs/C3N4-15, respectively. Upper-left and bottom-right insets in panel (c) correspond to an enlarged image of the selected CD and size distribution histogram of CDs, respectively. The inset in panel (d) is a magnified TEM image of selected area marked with red box, indicating a few CDs decorated on C3N4 nanosheets.
(a) XRD patterns and (b) absorption spectra of CDs, C3N4 and CDs/C3N4-15.
(a) Fluorescence spectra and (b) FTIR spectra of CDs, C3N4 and CDs/C3N4-15.
The XPS spectra of CDs/C3N4-15 after fluorescence detection for Cr6+. Wide scan spectra (a), high-resolution peaks for C 1s (b), N 1s (c) and Cr 2p (d).

(a) Fluorescence intensity of CDs/C3N4-15 as a function of Cr6+ concentration measured after 365 nm UV irradiation for 3 min under neutral conditions, and (b) the overall linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and Cr6+ concentration.
Fluorescence response of CDs (a) and C3N4 (b) as a function of Cr6+ concentration.

Fluorescent response of three probes to different ions of 80 μM measured after 365 nm UV irradiation for 3 min under neutral conditions: (a) CDs/C3N4-15, (b) CDs, and (c) C3N4. (d) SCr/SCu is defined as the sensitivity contrast of three probes for Cr6+ and Cu2+ of 80 μM, respectively.

(a) SCr/SCu results of CDs/C3N4, CDs and C3N4. (b) The effect of solution pH on the fluorescence response of CDs/C3N4-15 to Cr6+ of 80.0 μM. (c) Fluorescence response dynamics of CDs/C3N4-15 to Cr6+ of 80.0 μM. (d) Fluorescence intensity of CDs/C3N4-15 in NaCl solution of 10−5-1 M.
Schematic illustration of fluorescence response of CDs/C3N4 to Cr6+.
Response parameters of different CDs/C3N4 for Cr6+ detection.

Note: S is defined as S=
F 0
- F
F 0
* 100 % , and F0 and F are the fluorescent intensity of bare fluorescent probe and that in presence of 80 μM Cr6+, respectively. This definition is applicable throughout the article.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Summary of some CDs-based fluorescence probes for Cr6+ detection.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: SCr/SM is defined as the sensitivity contrast of CDs probes for Cr6+ and the reference ion with the second strongest fluorescence response. The optimal S and SCr/SM values are estimated from the graphical plot in references. IPDI, CTS and CD represent isophorone diisocyanate, chitosan and β-cyclodextrin, respectively.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Survey of Cr6+ detection of CDs/C3N4-15 in three real samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para:
Note: The measured Cr6+ concentration is calculated according to the linear relationship between peak intensity of CDs/C3N4-15 and Cr6+ concentration in Fig. 5b. Recovery(%)=(measured Cr6+ concentration/added one)×100 %. RSD is defined as RSD=
σ A
, and A and σ are the average and standard deviation of three measurements at certain Cr6+ concentration, respectively.

head.ce:title: Carbon dot/carbon nitride composites fluorescent probe for the highly selective detection of Cr(VI) ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Optoelectronic Science, Harbin Institute of Technology at Weihai, Weihai 264209, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Harbin Institute of Technology at Weihai

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Optoelectronic Science, Harbin Institute of Technology at Weihai, Weihai 264209, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150001, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence quenching of carbon dots (CDs) has been widely used for ions detection, but it usually exhibits poor selectivity for specific ions, so how to enhance their response selectivity is still an urgent task. In this paper, CDs and carbon nitride (C3N4) nanosheets are prepared by pyrolysis and thermal oxidation method, respectively, followed by their ultrasonic mixing to synthesize CDs/C3N4 composites. They are characterized by transmission electron microscope, X-ray diffractometer, X-ray photoelectron spectrometer, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, UV–vis and fluorescence spectrophotometer. The synthesized CDs and C3N4 nanosheets both exhibit strong fluorescence response to multiple ions. As a comparison, CDs/C3N4 composites exhibit highly enhanced selectivity to Cr6+ as a result of partial consumption of -NH2 groups and CN bonds or their reduced binding ability. Meanwhile, our CDs/C3N4 probe exhibits high sensitivity of 54 %, short response time of less than 1 min, low detection limit of 0.39 μM, wide linear response interval of 2.0−80.0 μM and high stability.

ce:section.ce:para: The detection of chromium ions (Cr6+) in drinking water is always an urgent task [1], due to their highly carcinogenic and mutagenic properties [2,3]. So the maximum Cr6+ concentration in drinking water is stipulated to be less than 100 ppb by US Environmental Protection Agency [4]. Practical Cr6+ detection method should satisfy “4S” standard (i.e., high sensitivity, high selectivity, high response speed and high stability). Besides, it should possess the advantages of low cost, convenient use and simple operation. Many analytical methods have been developed for accurate ion detection, such as electrochemical method [5], atomic absorption spectroscopy [6,7], adsorptive stripping voltammetry [8], liquid chromatography [9–11], and spectrophotometric method [12], but the extensive utilization of expensive instruments or environmental-unfriendly chemicals inhibits their practical application in various fields. As a contrast, fluorescence quenching method is considered as an appropriate alternative for heavy metal ion detection, and how to select apt fluorescent probe is vital. Carbon dots (CDs) are regarded as appropriate probes due to their high fluorescence efficiency, high stability and low cost [13–15].

At present, CDs have been successfully used for multiple ions detection, such as copper ion (Cu2+) [13,16], iron ion (Fe3+) [17,18], Cr6+ [19,20] and so on. But CDs often exhibit poor selectivity due to the simultaneous interaction between their functional groups and multiple ions [13]. Masking agent, heteroatom doping or functionalization has been utilized to enhance their selectivity for Cr6+ [2,21–23], but these methods have some inherent drawbacks. Masking method requires harsh experimental conditions, such as specific pH environment, colorless and low-solubility precipitates. Heteroatom doping often leads to poor crystallization, and it’s cumbersome to acquire the optimum doping amount [24,25]. For functionalization method, it’s usually challenging to accurately modify the surface of CDs and usually leads to weakened fluorescent signal [25,26]. Based on the above considerations, to construct CDs-based composites is an excellent alternative to enhance their selectivity by introducing a second fluorescent material [27,28]. As the star photocatalytic material, carbon nitride (C3N4) nanosheets also exhibit strong fluorescent response to metal ions [29]. So we envisage that the poor response selectivity of CDs can be overcome by simply constructing CDs/C3N4 composite.

Most chemical reagents were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Co., Shanghai, China, containing Polyethyleneimine (PEI), P-phenylenediamine, K2Cr2O7, AlCl3·6H2O, LiOH·6H2O, Mg(OH)2, Mn(CH3COO)2, KNO3, Sb(CH3COO)3, 3CdSO4·6H2O, CuSO4·5H2O, ZnSO4·7H2O, HgCl2 and Fe(NO3)3·9H2O. Melamine and ethylene glycol were purchased from Yongda Chemical Reagent Co., Tianjin, China. All chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Deionized (DI) water was used in all experiments.

Three steps were used to synthesize CDs/C3N4 composites. Firstly, CDs were synthesized according to the pyrolysis method described in our previous reference [30]. In a typical procedure, 0.5 g of PEI and 0.1 g of P-phenylenediamine were thoroughly dissolved in 12 mL of ethylene glycol solution containing 2 mL of DI water, followed by heating at 140 °C in an oil bath. After heating for about 1 h, the solution would form uniform gel and 2 mL of DI water was added to avoid its scorching. After this procedure repeated for 5 times, the formed sol was thoroughly dispersed in 20 mL of DI water, followed by neutralization with 1 M NaOH solution. The CDs solution was poured into ethanol solution to violently stir, followed by settling quietly for 24 h to remove the formed precipitates. Then, the ethanol was removed by heating at 80 °C. Lastly, the resulting solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min to further purify CDs, and the supernatant was selected as CDs solution. The corresponding CDs powder was obtained by drying the above solution in vacuum drying oven at 45 °C for 24 h for the subsequent characterization.

Secondly, C3N4 nanosheets were prepared by two-step thermal oxidation of melamine. In a typical procedure, 5 g of melamine was heated up to 550 °C in 1 h and lasted for 3 h in a muffle furnace. After natural cooling to room temperature, the synthesized pale yellow product was ground adequately and again heated at 510 °C for 3 h in air to acquire C3N4 nanosheets. After violent stirring in 100 mL of ethanol, the C3N4 was settled for 6 h, and the supernatant was removed to eliminate the unreacted salts and by-products. Then ethanol was replaced with DI water to repeat the above procedure for 2 times. The purified C3N4 solution was dried at 80 °C overnight in vacuum oven to prepare C3N4 powder. Finally, 1 mL of above purified CDs solution was ultrasonically dispersed into 20 mL of DI water for 30 min, followed by adding C3N4 of different amount with further ultrasonic dispersion for 2 h to acquire different CDs/C3N4 probes. For convenience, the CDs/C3N4 with x microgram of C3N4 nanosheets was denoted as CDs/C3N4-x. In the experiment, CDs/C3N4-5, -7.5, -15, -20, and -25 were synthesized to estimate the effect of C3N4 loading amount on their fluorescence response.

Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Zeiss MERLIN Compact), transmission electron microscopy (JEM-2100, JEOL) and X-ray diffractometer (XRD, DX-2700) with Cu kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å) were used to characterize the morphology and lattice structure of the prepared CDs, C3N4 and CDs/C3N4. X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were obtained by an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo ESCALAB 250 XI) to estimate the valance state of the samples. In addition, XPS spectra were also used to determine atom ratio of CDs and C3N4 in CDs/C3N4.Their functional groups were determined by a Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer in the range of 500−4000 cm−1 (NICOLET 380). Their optical properties were estimated by the UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV1800, SHIMADU) and fluorescence spectrometer (LED-YRGD, Suzhou Winray Optoelectronic Technology Co, Ltd).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of CDs/C3N4 probe for Cr6+
ce:section.ce:para: In order to measure the response of CDs/C3N4 to Cr6+, different amounts of Cr6+ were added into the CDs/C3N4 solution to obtain specific Cr6+ concentration, and their fluorescence response was measured by the fluorescence spectrometer. Meanwhile, CDs amount remained unchanged in different CDs/C3N4 probes. Due to the rapid decay of the fluorescence signal, the steady fluorescence measurement was performed after reaching their equilibrium (i.e., after 365 nm UV irradiation for 3 min.), except for estimating the fluorescence response dynamics of CDs/C3N4 to Cr6+. In addition, to estimate the selectivity of CDs/C3N4 probes for Cr6+, their fluorescence response to other reference metal and anion ions, such as Al3+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Li+, K+, Sb3+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Cl−, (CH3COO)−, SO4 2- and NO3 −, was also tested under the identical condition.

The QYs of CDs, C3N4 and CDs/C3N4 were calculated using the following equation with quinine sulfate (QY = 54 %) as standard reagent [30]. To minimize the reabsorption effect, their absorbance intensity was held below 0.100 at excitation wavelength.
Ф =
Ф '
×
A '
I '
×
I A
×
(
n
n '
)
2

Where Ф, A and I represent the QY, optical absorption intensity and integrated fluorescence intensity of probes, respectively. The symbols marked with apostrophe represent the corresponding parameters of quinine sulfate. n and n′ represent the refractive index of CDs and quinine sulfate solution, and are both equal to 1.33. To accurately measure the QY, averaging of four measurements results is used to reduce the measurement error. For CDs, the absorbance intensity in four measurements is 0.100, 0.094, 0.089 and 0.085, respectively, and the corresponding QY is 44 %, 44 %, 46 % and 42 %, respectively. For C3N4, the absorbance intensity is 0.083, 0.067, 0.053 and 0.051, respectively, and the corresponding QY is 45 %, 44 %, 43 % and 43 %, respectively. For CDs/C3N4-15, the absorbance intensity is 0.070, 0.064, 0.053 and 0.056, respectively, and its QY is 28 %, 28 %, 28 % and 27 %, respectively. So the QY of CDs, C3N4 and CDs/C3N4-15 is 44 %, 44 % and 28 %, respectively. The significantly reduced fluorescence of CDs/C3N4-15 means the good contact between CDs and C3N4 to inhibit the charge carrier recombination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cr6+ detection of CDs/C3N4 in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the reliability of our CDs/C3N4 probes to detect Cr6+ in real samples, three samples (i.e., lake water, tap water and sea water) were used in fluorescence tests. The lake water was obtained from Sun Moon Lake located at HIT (Weihai) campus. The tap water was collected from our lab. The sea water was taken from Yellow Sea near our campus. After filtering by the filter paper (0.22 μm) to remove impurities, three samples were used to prepare different ions solution with various concentrations.

As shown in Fig. 1 a, after two-step thermal oxidation, C3N4 nanosheets are formed and partially rolled up, and their sheet-like morphology is further confirmed by the corresponding TEM image in Fig. 1b. The formed CDs exhibit irregular spherical structure, and the upper-left enlarged picture of single CD indicates the evident lattice structure with lattice spacing of 0.29 nm (Fig. 1c). The bottom-right histogram indicates their narrow size distribution of 4.5–7.8 nm with an average of 6.2 nm. The upper-left enlarged image of CDs/C3N4 in Fig. 1d indicates a few CDs are successfully loaded on C3N4 nanosheets.

The XRD patterns of CDs, C3N4 and CDs/C3N4-15 are shown in Fig. 2 a. A broad peak at 21.4° of CDs corresponds to their (002) lattice plane [31]. The peaks at 13.1° and 27.6° of C3N4 correspond to its (100) and (002) planes, respectively [32]. For CDs/C3N4, the peaks of CDs and C3N4 remain unchanged, indicating their lattice structure is well preserved even after forming CDs/C3N4 composites. In addition, the weak peaks at 38.6° and 44.8° should come from the substrate.

The absorption spectra of CDs, C3N4 and CDs/C3N4-15 are shown in Fig. 2b. The peak at 368 nm of CDs is ascribed to the n-π* transition [31]. A wide absorption region at 200−390 nm of C3N4 is observed, corresponding to π-π* electronic transition in their s-triazine rings [33]. For CDs/C3N4, its UV absorption is evidently enhanced due to the synergistic effect between CDs and C3N4.

As shown in Fig. 3 a, CDs and C3N4 both exhibit strong fluorescence signal under 365 nm UV irradiation. Three emission peaks of CDs are located at 492, 513 and 586 nm, respectively, and two peaks of C3N4 are located at 444 and 465 nm, respectively. For CDs/C3N4-15, its fluorescence remains the characteristic peaks of CDs and C3N4.

FTIR spectra of CDs, C3N4 and CDs/C3N4 are shown in Fig. 3b. For CDs, the peaks at 1570 and 3270 cm−1 are assigned to the bending vibration and stretching vibration of -NH2 group, respectively [3,34]. The peaks at 1050 and 1650 cm−1 correspond to the CO vibration and stretching vibration of COO-, respectively [35,36]. For C3N4, the peaks at 1130 and 1540 cm-1 correspond to the CN and CN stretching vibrations, respectively, and remaining peaks in 1200-1620 cm-1 region are dominated by CNHC and CN stretching vibrations [37–39]. The peaks in 3080−3160 cm-1 region are attributed to amino and hydroxyl groups [40]. For CDs/C3N4, the peaks at 1570, 1650 and 3270 cm-1 of CDs are blue-shifted to 1580 cm-1, red-shifted to 1635 cm-1 and disappear, respectively. The peaks at 1230 and 1620 cm-1 of C3N4 are blue-shifted to 1250 and 1635 cm-1, respectively, and the peaks at 1400, 1540 and 3080−3160 cm-1 disappear. According to the different FTIR spectra of three probes, we should pay attention to the following two points: (1) the peaks at 3270 and 3160 cm-1 disappear, indicating that partial -NH2 groups of CDs and CN bonds of C3N4 have been consumed. (2) the peak at 1570 cm-1 of CDs is blue-shifted to 1580 cm-1, indicating the enhanced bond strength of partial -NH2 groups [41]. So it is further difficult for these -NH2 groups to combine with other reference ions.

The XPS spectra of CDs/C3N4-15 were used to estimate the valences of various elements, carbon ratio in CDs and C3N4 and valence change of detected Cr6+ (Fig. 4 ). Four predominant peaks at 285.24, 398.15, 530.59 and 576.41 eV correspond to C 1s, N 1s, O 1s and Cr 2p, respectively. The peaks at 283.9, 284.9 and 287.3 eV of C 1s correspond to CC/C=C of CDs, reference carbon and CN bonds of C3N4, respectively [42–44]. The ratio of carbon elements of CDs and C3N4 in CDs/C3N4-15 is estimated to be 0.28, and the results of other CDs/C3N4 are listed in Table S1 in the supporting information. The peaks at 398.1 and 400.2 eV of N 1s are assigned to CN bond and amino groups (i.e., -NH2 or = NH), respectively [45–47]. The peaks at 576.6 and 586.1 eV are assigned to Cr 2p3/2 and 2p1/2, respectively, indicating the effective reduction of Cr6+ to Cr3+ [48]. The oxygen element in CDs/C3N4 may come from the oxygen-containing functional groups or molecules (i.e., H2O and CO2) [49].

Now the fluorescence response of CDs/C3N4 probe to Cr6+ was estimated. CDs/C3N4-15 exhibits significant fluorescence attenuation with increasing Cr6+ concentration, which indicates their strong interaction (Fig. 5 a). Due to an analogous variation tendency of various fluorescence peaks as a function of Cr6+ concentration, the 468 nm peak is selected to characterize its fluorescence response to Cr6+. A good linear relationship (R2 = 0.998) between the fluorescence intensity and Cr6+ concentration is obtained in 2.0−80.0 μM (Fig. 5b). In addition, the small error bars illustrate the good repeatability of CDs/C3N4-15 for Cr6+ detection. The limit of detection (LOD) is defined as LOD=
3 σ
k
, and σ and k are the standard deviation of blank measurements and the corresponding slope of fitting curve, respectively. The values of σ and k of CDs, C3N4 and CDs/C3N4 are listed in Table S2 in the supporting information, and the LOD of CDs/C3N4-15 is estimated to be 0.39 μM.

Due to the strong fluorescent signal of CDs and C3N4, their fluorescence response to Cr6+ was also measured to estimate their role in the response of CDs/C3N4 (Fig. 6 ), and they both exhibited similar signal attenuation in the presence of Cr6+. The response parameters of CDs, C3N4 and CDs/C3N4-15 are listed in Table S3 in the supporting information. CDs/C3N4-15 exhibits the lowest LOD, best linear relationship and widest linear response interval among these three fluorescence probes except a little lower sensitivity than CDs.

The effect of C3N4 loading amount on the fluorescence response of CDs/C3N4 to Cr6+ was estimated (Fig. S1). Although these CDs/C3N4 probes exhibited different fluorescence spectra, their similar tendency was observed as a function of Cr6+ concentration. The response parameters are listed in Table 1 , and the C3N4 loading amount has a distinct effect on their fluorescence response to Cr6+. With the increase of C3N4 amount from 5 mg to 25 mg in CDs/C3N4, their sensitivity initially increases and then decreases and the LOD exhibits an opposite trend. With more than 15 mg of C3N4 loading in CDs/C3N4, the response interval reduces from 2.0 to 80.0 μM to 10.0−80.0 μM.

For the florescence probe, selectivity is used to indicate its anti-interference ability to detect specific ions, so it is an equally important parameter for practical sensors. The corresponding results of CDs/C3N4, CDs and C3N4 are shown in Fig. 7 and Fig. S2. CDs/C3N4-15 exhibits strong response to Cr6+ and obviously weakened response to other metal ions and anion ions. However, CDs and C3N4 simultaneously exhibit strong response to multiple metal ions. Specifically speaking, CDs exhibit strong response to Cr6+ and Cu2+, and C3N4 to Hg2+, Cr6+ and Cu2+, respectively. The small error bars illustrate the good repeatability of fluorescent probes to different ions. SCr/SCu is defined as the sensitivity contrast of three probes for Cr6+ and Cu2+ to quantitatively describe their selectivity, as shown in Fig. 7d. SCr/SCu of CDs/C3N4-15, CDs and C3N4 is 5.66, 1.38 and 0.66, respectively, which indicates the improved selectivity for Cr6+ is obtained for CDs/C3N4.

The effect of C3N4 loading amount in CDs/C3N4 on their selectivity for Cr6+ was also estimated, as shown in Fig. S3-S4. The effect of C3N4 loading amount on the response of CDs/C3N4 to anion ions is negligible. For various detected cations, CDs/C3N4-5 exhibits high sensitivity to Cr6+ and Cu2+ simultaneously, and negligible response to other ions. With the increase of C3N4 loading amount up to 7.5−20 mg, their response to Cu2+ is gradually inhibited, which leads to gradually enhanced selectivity of CDs/C3N4 for Cr6+. Excessive C3N4 loading (i.e., 25 mg) in CDs/C3N4 reduces its response sensitivity to Cr6+, which correspondingly lowers the selectivity of CDs/C3N4 for. Cr6+ The SCr/SCu values of CDs/C3N4 probes and corresponding CDs and C3N4 are shown in Fig. 8 a, and the supreme SCr/SCu of 9.48 is obtained for CDs/C3N4-20 probe.

Good fluorescence probes also require high stability and fast response speed, and the corresponding results are shown in Fig. 8. CDs/C3N4-15 exhibits strong fluorescence and high sensitivity under neutral conditions (Fig. 8b). In an acidic environment, its fluorescence is evidently lowered due to the formation of dichromate [50]. In an alkaline environment, its fluorescence is also obviously reduced, maybe due to the protonation or deprotonation of surface functional groups of CDs/C3N4 [51]. Meanwhile, its fluorescence response to Cr6+ decreases with increasing pH, therefore, all of the fluorescence detections are performed in the neutral environment. After adding 80 μM Cr6+, the fluorescence signal decays rapidly within 1 min (Fig. 8c), indicating its real-time detection ability for Cr6+. Meanwhile, its stability can be proved by its almost unchanged fluorescence after further 60 min UV irradiation. So all the experiments are performed after 365 nm UV irradiation for 3 min except measuring the dynamic response of CDs/C3N4 to Cr6+. The results in Fig. 8d indicate its strong adaptability under different ionic strength conditions.

Now we attempt to compare our CDs/C3N4 with reference results. During the fluorescence methods for ions detection, in addition to fluorescence quenching method, colorimetric and “turn-on” fluorescent methods were also used to detect Cr6+ [52,53]. In the colorimetric method, Cr6+ leaded to the change of localized surface plasmon spectrum of silver nanoparticles, but its relatively poor selectivity inhibited the wide application [52]. The “turn-on” method was also developed for highly sensitive Cr6+ detection, but some efforts were pursued to obtain wide response interval, high response speed and non-toxicity characteristics [53].

As comparison, CDs-based fluorescence quenching method was used to high delicately detect Cr6+. Their selectivity can be efficiently improved by the addition of masking agents, heteroatom doping, functionalization or construction of compound nanostructures [3,21–23,27,28,54–56], and their performance parameters are listed in Table 2 to intuitively compare with our results. The addition of masking agents can improve the selectivity for Cr6+ by forming stable coordination compound with reference ions [3,54]. However, the use of toxic masking agents inhibits their extensive application. Heteroatom doping can also improve the response performance of CDs, but it is relatively difficult to simultaneously obtain high sensitivity and selectivity [21,22]. Functionalized CDs usually suffer from poor selectivity or cumbersome functional process [23,55]. CDs-based composites can detect Cr6+ with high sensitivity, but many efforts are needed to obtain satisfactory selectivity [27,28,54]. As a comparison, our CDs/C3N4 probes exhibit relatively high selectivity and sensitivity for Cr6+. Especially, the non-toxicity and low-cost properties of CDs and C3N4 ensure the wide application of CDs/C3N4 probes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cr6+ detection of CDs/C3N4-15 in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The detection results of CDs/C3N4-15 for Cr6+ in three real samples are listed in Table 3 . In details, the concentration of measured Cr6+ is close to the added ones and the final recoveries are between 90.7 % and 104.8 %. Meanwhile, the relative standard deviation (RSD) is in the range of 0.8–2.7 %. So, the negligible interference effect can be ignored in Cr6+ detection, which indicates that our CDs/C3N4 probes can be used in the real water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response mechanism of CDs/C3N4 to Cr6+
ce:section.ce:para: Owe to the decisive role of surface functional groups in the fluorescence response of CDs/C3N4 probe to Cr6+, FTIR technology was used to estimate the interaction of Cr6+ with CDs/C3N4, CDs and C3N4 (Fig. S5). For CDs, the presence of Cr6+ or Cu2+ leads to the blue-shift of 1050 cm−1 peak (CO) or blue-shift or disappear of the peaks at 1570 and 3270 cm−1 (-NH2), respectively, indicating the strong fluorescence response of CDs to Cr6+ and Cu2+ by CO bonds and -NH2 groups, respectively. For C3N4, the presence of Cr6+ or Cu2+ leads to disappear of 1130 cm−1 peak (CN) or red-shift of 1540 cm−1 peak (CN), respectively, indicating the strong fluorescence response of C3N4 to Cr6+ and Cu2+ by CN bonds and CN bonds, respectively. For CDs/C3N4, in presence of Cr6+, the peaks at 1050 cm−1 (CO) and 1130 cm −1 (CN) blue-shift and disappear, respectively, indicating the interaction of Cr6+ with CO and CN bonds of CDs/C3N4, which is consistent with the results of CDs and C3N4. In a word, the CO of CDs and CN bonds of C3N4 determine the fluorescence response of CDs/C3N4 to Cr6+ [2,57].

The response process of CDs/C3N4 to Cr6+ is shown in Scheme 1 . After the addition of Cr6+, it is preferentially combined with CO or CN bonds of CDs/C3N4 [58]. Under 365 nm UV irradiation, electron-hole pairs are generated in CDs/C3N4, and electrons will transfer to the absorbed Cr6+ on CDs/C3N4 surface by CO and CN bonds, followed by the reduction of Cr6+ to Cr3+, considering the redox potential of Cr6+/Cr3+ is more positive than conduction band of CDs and C3N4 [59]. So Cr6+ can be successfully reduced into Cr3+ during the fluorescence detection [2]. Therefore, this interaction between Cr6+ and CDs/C3N4 results in nonradiative electron/hole recombination, and correspondingly the fluorescence quenching of CDs/C3N4 probe.

Now we discuss the reasons for the enhanced selectivity of our CDs/C3N4. As shown in Fig. S5, the -NH2 groups or CN bonds of CDs and C3N4 can combine with Cu2+, leading to their strong fluorescence response to Cu2+. For CDs/C3N4, due to partially consumption of -NH2 groups and CN bonds or their reduced binding ability it exhibits obviously weakened response to Cu2+ and corresponding enhanced selectivity for Cr6+, which can be proved by the blue-shift or disappearance of peaks of -NH2 groups and blue-shift of peak of CN bonds (Fig. 3b). Meanwhile, the strong fluorescence quenching for CDs/C3N4-Mn+ (M: metal) system requires efficient electron transfer from the excited state of CDs/C3N4 to Mn+. From the standard formal potential (E0) of Mn+/M, the redox potential of Cr6+/Cr3+ is possibly optimum for the electron transfer from CDs/C3N4 to other reference ions [58].

The inner filter effect (IFE) of Cr6+ is also an important factor for the fluorescence response of CDs [4,60], so it needs to be evaluated for our CDs/C3N4 probes. As shown in Fig. S6, Cr6+ solution of 80 μM exhibits a weak absorption peak at 368 nm, overlapping with the excitation wavelength (i.e., 365 nm), so the IFE of Cr6+ can theoretically lead to fluorescence attenuation. But the absorption intensity of 80 μM Cr6+ solution accounts for merely 20 % of that of our CDs/C3N4, so IFE is the secondary factor. Especially considering the adjusted Cr6+ concentration throughout the experiment is usually far less than 80 μM, so IFE effect can reasonably be ignored for the fluorescence response of CDs/C3N4 probes.

In summary, CDs and C3N4 nanosheets were synthesized by the pyrolysis and two-step thermal oxidation method, respectively, followed by their ultrasonic mixing to form CDs/C3N4 composite probe. Due to decoration with rich functional groups, CDs and C3N4 both exhibit strong fluorescence response to multiple ions. As a comparison, enhanced selectivity for Cr6+ is obtained for CDs/C3N4 probes. According to fluorescence response of CDs/C3N4 to Cr6+, we think that Cr6+ is absorbed by CO and CN bonds on CDs/C3N4 surface and then reduced into Cr3+ by the photo-generated electrons in CDs/C3N4, leading to the florescence quenching. Meanwhile, due to partial consumption of -NH2 groups and CN bonds or their reduced binding ability to Cu2+, the enhanced selectivity of CDs/C3N4 for Cr6+ is obtained. Meanwhile, CDs/C3N4 exhibits high sensitivity of 54 %, short response time of less than 1 min, low LOD of 0.39 μM, wide response interval of 2.0−80.0 μM, good biocompatibility and high stability.

We have revised the Manuscript titled “Carbon dot/carbon nitride composites fluorescent probe for the highly selective detection of Cr(VI) ions” (ID: JPHOTOCHEM-D-20−00223R1) according to the reviewer’s comments. The relevant description has been marked with red font throughout the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we do not have any commercial or associative interest that represents a conflict of interest in connection with the work submitted.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by HIT(WH)-Weihai Co-construction Project (Grant No. ITGAZMZ001702).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2020.112711.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Dong, Carbon dots with red emission as a fluorescent and colorimeteric dual-readout probe for the detection of chromium (vi) and cysteine and its logic gate operation, J. Mater. Chem. B 6 (2018) 6099-6107.

Sens. Actuators B Chem.
